## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
*Define standard test signals. List any four standard test signals used in control system analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Standard test signals are well-defined mathematical input functions applied to a control system to study its time-domain and frequency-domain behavior. They provide a standardized, reproducible benchmark for comparing different systems. The four standard test signals are:
>
> 1. **Unit Impulse signal** $\delta(t)$
> 2. **Unit Step signal** $u(t)$
> 3. **Unit Ramp signal** $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$
> 4. **Unit Parabolic (Acceleration) signal** $p(t) = \dfrac{t^2}{2} \cdot u(t)$
>
> (A fifth, sinusoidal signal, may also be listed.)
>
> *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing 4 signals + 1 mark for mathematical expressions.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
*State the Laplace transform of the unit step, unit ramp, and unit impulse signals.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Signal | Laplace Transform |
|---|---|
| Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$ | $1$ |
| Unit Step $u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ |
| Unit Ramp $t \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ |

*(1 mark per correct entry.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the Laplace transform of the unit ramp signal $r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ using the definition of the Laplace transform. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the relationship between unit impulse, unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic signals using differentiation and integration. Show their Laplace transforms. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\}$:**

Starting from the bilateral Laplace transform definition:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt
$$

Apply integration by parts. Let:

$$
u_1 = t \quad \Rightarrow \quad du_1 = dt
$$

$$
dv_1 = e^{-st} dt \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = -\frac{e^{-st}}{s}
$$

Substituting into $\int u_1\, dv_1 = u_1 v_1 - \int v_1\, du_1$:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \left[ t \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) \right]_0^{\infty} - \int_0^{\infty} \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) dt
$$

Evaluate the boundary term (assuming $\text{Re}(s) > 0$, $e^{-st} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$):

$$
\left[ -\frac{t \cdot e^{-st}}{s} \right]_0^{\infty} = 0 - 0 = 0
$$

The remaining integral:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s} \int_0^{\infty} e^{-st} dt = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s^2}}
$$

*(Award: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for integration by parts, 2 marks for final evaluation.)*

---

**(b) Relationship between standard test signals:**

**Differentiation chain (signal becomes sharper):**

$$
p(t) = \frac{t^2}{2} u(t) \quad \xrightarrow{d/dt} \quad r(t) = t\,u(t) \quad \xrightarrow{d/dt} \quad u(t) \quad \xrightarrow{d/dt} \quad \delta(t)
$$

**Integration chain (signal becomes smoother):**

$$
\delta(t) \quad \xrightarrow{\int dt} \quad u(t) \quad \xrightarrow{\int dt} \quad t \cdot u(t) \quad \xrightarrow{\int dt} \quad \frac{t^2}{2} u(t)
$$

**Verification using Laplace transform (since differentiation $\to$ multiplication by $s$ in $s$-domain):**

- $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s} \Rightarrow s \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} = 1 = \mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\}$ Ō£ō
- $\mathcal{L}\{t\,u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s^2} \Rightarrow s \cdot \dfrac{1}{s^2} = \dfrac{1}{s} = \mathcal{L}\{u(t)\}$ Ō£ō
- $\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)\right\} = \dfrac{1}{s^3} \Rightarrow s \cdot \dfrac{1}{s^3} = \dfrac{1}{s^2} = \mathcal{L}\{t\,u(t)\}$ Ō£ō

**Tabulated Summary:**

| Signal | $x(t)$ | $X(s)$ | Relation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Impulse | $\delta(t)$ | $1$ | $d(u)/dt$ |
| Step | $u(t)$ | $1/s$ | $\int \delta\, dt$ |
| Ramp | $t\,u(t)$ | $1/s^2$ | $\int u\, dt$ |
| Parabolic | $\dfrac{t^2}{2}u(t)$ | $1/s^3$ | $\int r\, dt$ |

*(Award: 3 marks for differentiation chain, 2 marks for integration chain, 2 marks for Laplace verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö [Alternative Choice for the same slot]**

**(a)** A control system has a transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$. Determine the response of the system when the input is (i) unit impulse and (ii) unit step. Sketch both responses qualitatively. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define standard test signals. Explain with a real-world example why the unit ramp signal is used to test Type-1 control systems. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) System responses:**

Given $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$.

**(i) Unit Impulse Response:**

For impulse input, $R(s) = 1$, so $C(s) = G(s) \cdot 1 = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$.

Apply partial fraction expansion:

$$
\frac{10}{s(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+2}
$$

Solving: $A = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5$ (multiply by $s$, set $s=0$), and $B = \dfrac{10}{-2} = -5$ (multiply by $s+2$, set $s=-2$).

$$
C(s) = \frac{5}{s} - \frac{5}{s+2}
$$

Take inverse Laplace transform:

$$
c(t) = 5\left(1 - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)
$$

**Qualitative sketch:** Starts at $c(0) = 0$, rises exponentially, and asymptotically approaches $c(\infty) = 5$ with time constant $\tau = 1/2 = 0.5$ seconds.

**(ii) Unit Step Response:**

For step input, $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$, so:

$$
C(s) = \frac{10}{s^2(s+2)}
$$

Apply partial fraction expansion:

$$
\frac{10}{s^2(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+2}
$$

Multiply through by $s^2(s+2)$:

$$
10 = A s(s+2) + B(s+2) + C s^2
$$

Set $s = 0$: $10 = 2B \Rightarrow B = 5$.
Set $s = -2$: $10 = 4C \Rightarrow C = 2.5$.
Compare $s^2$ coefficients: $0 = A + C \Rightarrow A = -2.5$.

So:

$$
C(s) = -\frac{2.5}{s} + \frac{5}{s^2} + \frac{2.5}{s+2}
$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$
c(t) = \left(-2.5 + 5t + 2.5 e^{-2t}\right) u(t)
$$

**Qualitative sketch:** A linear ramp $5t$ with a transient exponential correction $2.5e^{-2t}$ and a constant offset $-2.5$. The system tracks a ramp with a **finite steady-state error of $2.5$ units** ŌĆö confirming it is a Type-1 system.

*(Award: 2 marks impulse setup + 2 marks PFE + 1 mark inverse Laplace + 2 marks step PFE/inverse.)*

---

**(b) Real-world use of unit ramp for Type-1 systems:**

**Definition:** Standard test signals are well-defined input functions (impulse, step, ramp, parabolic, sinusoidal) applied to a control system to analyze its time-domain behavior in a standardized manner.

**Why ramp for Type-1 systems?**

A Type-1 system has one pure integrator in its open-loop transfer function. Mathematically, this gives the closed-loop transfer function a steady-state error of **zero for a step input** but a **finite, constant error for a ramp input**.

**Real-world example ŌĆö Radar antenna tracking:**

Consider an air-traffic-control radar that must track an aircraft climbing at a **constant elevation rate** (linear angle vs. time). The desired antenna position is a **ramp function** of time.

- The radar control system is a Type-1 system (one integrator ensures zero steady-state error to position).
- When the aircraft climbs at a constant rate, the antenna must follow a ramp input.
- A Type-1 system will track this ramp with a **constant angular lag** (steady-state error), but the error will not grow unbounded ŌĆö it remains bounded and predictable.

Engineers use the **ramp response** to compute this constant lag (the **velocity error constant** $K_v$), which is critical for radar performance specifications. The formula used is:

$$
e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \lim_{s \to 0} s R(s) G(s), \quad R(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}
$$

*(Award: 2 marks definition + 2 marks Type-1 explanation + 2 marks radar example + 1 mark velocity error constant formula.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit $u(t)$:** Students often write the ramp as $r(t) = t$ without $u(t)$, losing 0.5 mark. Always include the unit step multiplier.
> 2. **Region of Convergence:** For full marks on the Laplace derivation, **state explicitly** that $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ is required for convergence.
> 3. **Poles at origin:** When computing step response of $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+2)}$, the pole at $s=0$ means the step response has a ramp component. **Do not forget the $5t$ term** in the partial fraction expansion.
> 4. **Drawing the waveform:** When asked to "sketch," you must draw a labelled graph. A textual description alone gets only 50% credit.
> 5. **Mixing impulse with step:** Many students confuse $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = s$ with $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$. The correct identity is $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$, and the derivative of the step is the impulse.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Standard Test Signals (KTU Module 3):**

- ŌŁÉ **Five canonical signals:** Impulse, Step, Ramp, Parabolic, Sinusoid.
- ŌŁÉ **Memorize the Laplace transforms:** $\delta(t) \to 1$, $u(t) \to 1/s$, $t \to 1/s^2$, $t^2/2 \to 1/s^3$.
- ŌŁÉ **The calculus chain:** $\delta \to u \to r \to p$ (each is the integral of the previous).
- ŌŁÉ **Impulse response = inverse Laplace of $G(s)$** (most powerful characterization).
- ŌŁÉ **Step response** ŌåÆ rise time, peak time, settling time, overshoot, steady-state error.
- ŌŁÉ **Ramp response** ŌåÆ velocity error constant $K_v$ for Type-1 systems.
- ŌŁÉ **Parabolic response** ŌåÆ acceleration error constant $K_a$ for Type-2 systems.
- ŌŁÉ **Sinusoidal response** ŌåÆ frequency response $G(j\omega)$, magnitude/phase Bode plots.
- ŌŁÉ **Convergence condition:** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ for all polynomial-type test signals.
- ŌŁÉ **Sifting property:** $\int f(t)\delta(t-a) dt = f(a)$ ŌĆö frequently tested.
- ŌŁÉ **Delayed versions:** $\mathcal{L}\{u(t-T)\} = e^{-sT}/s$, $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-T)\} = e^{-sT}$.
- ŌŁÉ **Why standard?** Reproducibility, mathematical simplicity, Laplace tractability, fair benchmarking.
- ŌŁÉ **KTU pattern:** Any time-response derivation begins with finding $C(s) = G(s) \cdot R(s)$ and inverting ŌĆö so test-signal Laplace transforms are step **zero** of every problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type and Order of Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Type and Order of Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Order" of a System

In control system engineering, the **Order** of a linear time-invariant (LTI) system is defined as the value of the highest power of the complex frequency variable **$s$** in the denominator polynomial of the **Closed-Loop Transfer Function (CLTF)**. This denominator is fundamentally known as the **Characteristic Equation** of the system.

For a general closed-loop transfer function expressed as:
$$T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{b_0 s^m + b_1 s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_m}{a_0 s^n + a_1 s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n}$$

The order of the system is **$n$**, where $n > m$ is a fundamental requirement for a system to be physically realizable (causal and proper).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The order is determined solely from the **closed-loop** characteristic polynomial, NOT from the open-loop transfer function.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of "Type" of a System

The **Type** (or system type number, $N$) of a control system is defined as the number of **pure integrations** (i.e., poles at the origin, $s = 0$) present in the **Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)**, denoted as $G(s)H(s)$.

If the open-loop transfer function is expressed in standard time-constant form as:
$$G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(1 + \tau_1 s)(1 + \tau_2 s)\cdots(1 + \tau_m s)}{s^N (1 + T_1 s)(1 + T_2 s)\cdots(1 + T_n s)}$$

Then the system is classified as:
- **Type 0 System** if $N = 0$ (No integrators)
- **Type 1 System** if $N = 1$ (One integrator)
- **Type 2 System** if $N = 2$ (Two integrators)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy: The Cruise Control System of a Car**

Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant speed (analogous to a **step input**):

- **Type 0 System (No Integrator):** Think of a basic mechanical governor. When you press the gas pedal, the car speeds up, but it might settle at a speed slightly **below or above** your target due to a constant disturbance (e.g., a slight incline). There is a small, permanent **steady-state error** because the system does not "remember" the past error and accumulate a correction.

- **Type 1 System (One Integrator):** Think of a modern adaptive cruise control. If the car slows down due to a hill, the system "integrates" the error over time, gradually increasing fuel until the speed exactly matches the target. The error eventually becomes **zero** for a step input, but for a **constantly changing** target (ramp input), there is a finite, non-zero error.

- **Type 2 System (Two Integrators):** Think of a highly advanced autopilot predicting a curved road. It integrates twice, allowing it to track a **parabolic trajectory** (like an accelerating target) with zero error.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The Type number tells you **how well the system handles sustained or growing inputs**, while the Order tells you **how complex the dynamic response** (transient oscillations, settling) will be.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Systems Based on Type

The "Type" is a property exclusively of the **Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF)** $G(s)H(s)$, but its importance is profound: it directly dictates the **steady-state error ($e_{ss}$)** of the closed-loop system for standard test inputs.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Determining Type:
1. Obtain the OLTF: $G(s)H(s)$.
2. Rewrite the OLTF in the **Bode / Time-Constant form** (factoring out the gains and time constants).
3. Count the number of poles lying exactly at $s = 0$.
4. This count is the **Type Number** ($N$).

## 2.2 Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$) Analysis using Final Value Theorem

The steady-state error is computed using the **Error Transfer Function** and the **Final Value Theorem (FVT)**:

$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

## 2.3 Static Error Constants (KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet)

| Error Constant | Definition (Formula) | Physical Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Position Error Constant ($K_p$)** | $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$ | Ability to track a **step (position)** input | Dimensionless |
| **Velocity Error Constant ($K_v$)** | $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s)$ | Ability to track a **ramp (velocity)** input | $\text{sec}^{-1}$ |
| **Acceleration Error Constant ($K_a$)** | $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s)H(s)$ | Ability to track a **parabolic (acceleration)** input | $\text{sec}^{-2}$ |

## 2.4 Comprehensive $e_{ss}$ Formula Table (KTU Board Favorite)

| Input $r(t)$ | Input $R(s)$ | Type 0 System | Type 1 System | Type 2 System |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Step Input** (Unit) | $\frac{1}{s}$ | $\frac{1}{1+K_p}$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Ramp Input** ($t$) | $\frac{1}{s^2}$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{1}{K_v}$ | $0$ |
| **Parabolic Input** ($\frac{t^2}{2}$) | $\frac{1}{s^3}$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\frac{1}{K_a}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In robotic arm position control, a **Type 1** system is typically used because we need zero error when holding a fixed position. In a satellite tracking antenna, a **Type 2** system is preferred to track moving targets (like satellites orbiting the earth) with minimal error.

## 2.5 Determining the Order (Step-by-Step)

1. Write the closed-loop transfer function: $T(s) = \frac{C(s)}{R(s)}$.
2. Identify the denominator: $\Delta(s) = 1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$ (Characteristic Equation).
3. Find the highest power of $s$ in $\Delta(s)$. This is the order $n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Error for a Type 0 System (Step Input)

**Problem Statement:** Given a unity feedback system with OLTF $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+3)(s+5)}$, determine the type, order, and steady-state error for a unit step input.

**Step 1: Identify the Type**
Looking at the OLTF, the denominator has one factor of $s$ (one pole at origin). 

$$\text{Type } N = 1 \quad \text{(Type 1 System)}$$

The order must be determined from the **closed-loop** transfer function.

**Step 2: Calculate $K_p$**
For a Type 1 system, the position error constant $K_p$ is:
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+3)(s+5)} = \frac{2K}{0} = \infty$$

**Step 3: Apply Final Value Theorem for Step Input**
The error transfer function in unity feedback is:
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{s}}{1 + \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+3)(s+5)}}$$

$$E(s) = \frac{s(s+3)(s+5)}{s(s+3)(s+5) + K(s+2)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{(s+3)(s+5)}{s(s+3)(s+5) + K(s+2)}$$

Applying Final Value Theorem:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{1/s}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Error for a Type 1 System (Ramp Input)

**Problem Statement:** For the same Type 1 system, find $e_{ss}$ for a unit ramp input $r(t) = t$.

**Step 1: Calculate $K_v$**
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K(s+2)}{s(s+3)(s+5)}$$

$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{K(s+2)}{(s+3)(s+5)} = \frac{K(0+2)}{(0+3)(0+5)} = \frac{2K}{15}$$

**Step 2: Apply Final Value Theorem for Ramp Input**
For a unit ramp, $R(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}$.
$$E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{1}{s^2}}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$$

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s \cdot \frac{1}{s^2}}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s \cdot G(s)H(s)} = \frac{1}{K_v}$$

$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{2K/15} = \frac{15}{2K}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Determining the Order of the System

**Given:** $G(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+4)}$ and $H(s) = 1$ (Unity Feedback).

**Step 1: Form the Closed-Loop Transfer Function**
$$T(s) = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} = \frac{\frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}{1 + \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+4)}}$$

$$T(s) = \frac{10}{s(s+2)(s+4) + 10}$$

**Step 2: Expand the Denominator (Characteristic Equation)**
$$\begin{aligned}
s(s+2)(s+4) + 10 &= s(s^2 + 6s + 8) + 10 \\
&= s^3 + 6s^2 + 8s + 10
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Highest Power**
The highest power of $s$ in the denominator is **$s^3$**.

$$\boxed{\text{Order} = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Type} = 1}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Type Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the hierarchical classification of control systems based on their Open-Loop Transfer Function (OLTF) structure, explicitly showing how the number of poles at the origin determines the Type.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Open-Loop Transfer Function G(s)H(s)"]:::startNode --> B{"Count poles at s = 0"};
    B -- "N = 0" --> C["Type 0 System"];
    B -- "N = 1" --> D["Type 1 System"];
    B -- "N = 2" --> E["Type 2 System"];
    B -- "N >= 3" --> F["Type N System (Rare, Unstable)"]:::warnNode;

    C --> G["Steady State Error for Step is Finite"];
    C --> H["Steady State Error for Ramp is Infinite"];
    C --> I["Steady State Error for Parabolic is Infinite"];

    D --> J["Steady State Error for Step is Zero"];
    D --> K["Steady State Error for Ramp is Finite"];
    D --> L["Steady State Error for Parabolic is Infinite"];

    E --> M["Steady State Error for Step is Zero"];
    E --> N["Steady State Error for Ramp is Zero"];
    E --> O["Steady State Error for Parabolic is Finite"];

    classDef startNode fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef warnNode fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Error Signal Flow in Unity Feedback

The following schematic illustrates the computation of the steady-state error $e_{ss}$ by tracing the error signal from the comparator to the output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    R["Input R(s)"]:::signalNode --> SUM(("Comparator: Summing Junction")):::sumNode;
    SUM -- "Error Signal E(s)" --> G["G(s): Forward Path Controller / Plant"]:::plantNode;
    G --> C["Output C(s)"]:::signalNode;
    C --> H["H(s) = 1: Unity Feedback Path"]:::feedbackNode;
    H -- "Feedback Signal" --> SUM;
    
    E_CALC["E(s) = R(s) / 1 + G(s)H(s)"]:::mathNode -.-> SUM;

    classDef signalNode fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef sumNode fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000;
    classDef plantNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef feedbackNode fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef mathNode fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5;
```

## 4.3 Order Determination Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1["Step 1: Obtain CLTF T(s) = G(s) / 1 + G(s)H(s)"]:::step1Node
    P2["Step 2: Cross multiply to form 1 + G(s)H(s) = 0"]:::step2Node
    P3["Step 3: Expand denominator polynomial in s"]:::step3Node
    P4["Step 4: Find highest power of s. This is the ORDER"]:::step4Node

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4

    classDef step1Node fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef step2Node fill:#c5cae9,stroke:#283593,color:#000
    classDef step3Node fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,color:#000
    classDef step4Node fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Type" of a control system. How does the type number affect the steady-state error for different standard inputs?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Type** of a control system is defined as the number of pure integrations (poles at the origin $s=0$) present in the **open-loop transfer function** $G(s)H(s)$. It is denoted by $N$.

**Effect on Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):**
- A **Type 0** system has a finite, non-zero error for a step input, but infinite error for ramp and parabolic inputs.
- A **Type 1** system has zero error for a step input, finite error for a ramp input, and infinite error for a parabolic input.
- A **Type 2** system has zero error for both step and ramp inputs, and finite error for a parabolic input.
- Generally, increasing the Type number by 1 reduces the steady-state error by one order for polynomial inputs.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between the "Type" and "Order" of a control system with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Type | Order |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Number of pure integrators in the OLTF. | Highest power of $s$ in the denominator of the CLTF. |
| **Determined From** | Open-Loop Transfer Function $G(s)H(s)$. | Closed-Loop Transfer Function $T(s)$. |
| **Significance** | Determines steady-state accuracy. | Determines the dynamic complexity (number of energy storage elements). |

**Example:** For $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(s+2)(s+5)}$, the system has **Type 1** (one pole at origin). The closed-loop characteristic equation is $s^3 + 7s^2 + 10s + K = 0$, so the **Order is 3**.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) - Module Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** For a unity feedback control system with open-loop transfer function $G(s) = \frac{10(s+1)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}$, determine:
   (i) The Type and Order of the system. (4 Marks)
   (ii) The steady-state error for a unit step input. (3 Marks)

**(b)** Explain the concept of static error constants ($K_p$, $K_v$, $K_a$) and compute them for the system given in part (a). For a unit ramp input, determine the steady-state error. (7 Marks)

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) (i) - Type and Order:**
The OLTF is $G(s)H(s) = \frac{10(s+1)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}$.
Counting poles at $s=0$: There are **two** poles at the origin ($s^2$ in denominator).
$$\text{Type} = 2$$
The closed-loop characteristic equation is $1 + G(s)H(s) = 0$:
$$s^2(s+5)(s+10) + 10(s+1) = 0$$
Expanding: $s^4 + 15s^3 + 50s^2 + 10s + 10 = 0$.
The highest power is $s^4$.
$$\text{Order} = 4$$

**Part (a) (ii) - Steady-state error for Unit Step Input:**
For a Type 2 system, $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \infty$.
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + \infty} = 0$$

**Part (b) - Static Error Constants and Ramp Error:**
**Static Error Constants:**
- **Position Constant $K_p$:** $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \frac{10(1)}{0} = \infty$
- **Velocity Constant $K_v$:** $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+1)}{s(s+5)(s+10)} = \frac{10(1)}{\infty} = 0$

Wait, this requires re-evaluation. For $G(s) = \frac{10(s+1)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}$:
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10(s+1)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+1)}{s(s+5)(s+10)} = \frac{10}{\infty} = 0$$

- **Acceleration Constant $K_a$:** 
$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot \frac{10(s+1)}{s^2(s+5)(s+10)} = \frac{10(1)}{(5)(10)} = \frac{10}{50} = 0.2$$

**Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp Input:**
For a unit ramp input, $R(s) = \frac{1}{s^2}$.
$$e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$$

**Verification using the general rule:** A Type 2 system has zero error for a ramp input, but $K_v$ here gives 0, which mathematically means infinite error. This is a **contradiction** that students must catch! The error here is that the standard rule "Type 2 ŌåÆ Zero ramp error" applies **only** if the OLTF has a pure $1/s^2$ term. Since this system has a zero at $s = -1$ which cancels part of the dynamics (or in this case, simply that the system has a non-unity numerator), the strict textbook Type-2 rule applies. 

*Correction:* For a standard Type 2 system $\frac{K}{s^2}$, $K_v = 0$ and $e_{ss}$ (ramp) $= 0$ because we evaluate the limit properly:
$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{1}{s \cdot G(s)H(s)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{s^2(s+5)(s+10)}{10(s+1)} = \frac{0}{10} = 0$$

So the **correct ramp error is 0**. The formula $1/K_v$ is a shortcut. The final answer for ramp is $e_{ss} = 0$.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** A unity feedback system has $G(s) = \frac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+2s)}$. Determine:
   (i) The Type and Order. (3 Marks)
   (ii) The value of $K$ such that the steady-state error for a unit ramp input is $0.1$. (4 Marks)

**(b)** For a system with $G(s)H(s) = \frac{50}{(1+0.1s)(1+2s)}$, compute the steady-state errors for unit step, unit ramp, and unit parabolic inputs. (7 Marks)

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) (i):**
OLTF: $G(s)H(s) = \frac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+2s)}$. 
There is **one** pole at origin.
$$\text{Type} = 1$$
Characteristic equation: $s(1+0.5s)(1+2s) + K = 0 \Rightarrow 0.5s \cdot 2s^2 \text{ terms} \Rightarrow s^3 \text{ is the highest power}$.
$$\text{Order} = 3$$

**Part (a) (ii):**
For a unit ramp input, $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = 0.1 \Rightarrow K_v = 10$.
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s)H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K}{s(1+0.5s)(1+2s)} = K$$
Therefore, $K = 10$.

**Part (b):**
OLTF: $G(s)H(s) = \frac{50}{(1+0.1s)(1+2s)}$.
There are **zero** poles at origin.
$$\text{Type} = 0$$

**Error Constants:**
$$K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{50}{(1+0.1s)(1+2s)} = \frac{50}{(1)(1)} = 50$$
$$K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{50}{(1+0.1s)(1+2s)} = 0$$
$$K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 \cdot \frac{50}{(1+0.1s)(1+2s)} = 0$$

**Steady-State Errors:**
- **Unit Step Input:** $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{1 + K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + 50} = \frac{1}{51} \approx 0.0196$
- **Unit Ramp Input:** $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_v} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$
- **Unit Parabolic Input:** $e_{ss} = \frac{1}{K_a} = \frac{1}{0} = \infty$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Type vs. Order Confusion:** A very common mistake is to determine the Order from the OLTF. The **Order** comes from the **Closed-Loop** denominator. The **Type** comes from the **Open-Loop** denominator. Mixing these up results in **zero marks**.
> 2. **Missing the "Unity Feedback" Assumption:** The standard error formulas ($1/(1+K_p)$, $1/K_v$, $1/K_a$) apply **only** for unity negative feedback systems ($H(s) = 1$). If $H(s) \neq 1$, you must compute the error transfer function $E(s) = \frac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ explicitly.
> 3. **Final Value Theorem Validity:** The FVT requires that all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the left half of the s-plane. Using FVT on an unstable system gives **invalid (and penalized)** answers.
> 4. **Units in Error Constants:** Forgetting to state the units ($\text{sec}^{-1}$ for $K_v$, $\text{sec}^{-2}$ for $K_a$) may cost you 0.5 to 1 mark in long-answer evaluations.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **[!]** **Type ($N$):** Counted from the **Open-Loop** transfer function. It is the exponent of $s$ in the denominator that equals zero (poles at the origin).
- **[!]** **Order ($n$):** Counted from the **Closed-Loop** transfer function denominator (characteristic equation). It is the highest power of $s$.
- **[!]** **Type 0:** Finite step error, infinite ramp and parabolic errors.
- **[!]** **Type 1:** Zero step error, finite ramp error ($e_{ss} = 1/K_v$), infinite parabolic error.
- **[!]** **Type 2:** Zero step and ramp errors, finite parabolic error ($e_{ss} = 1/K_a$).
- **[!]** The static error constants are defined as $K_p = \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)$, $K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} sG(s)H(s)$, and $K_a = \lim_{s \to 0} s^2 G(s)H(s)$.
- **[!]** The Final Value Theorem is the core mathematical tool: $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{sR(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$.
- **[!]** **Practical Rule:** To increase system Type, add integrators (e.g., add a PI controller). This reduces steady-state error but may compromise transient stability (Root Locus shifts right).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Poles and Zeros
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT631 - NETWORK AND LINEAR CONTROL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Concept of Poles and Zeros # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the analysis of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI)** systems modeled under the **Laplace Transform** framework, every physically realizable system can be characterized by a mathematical construct known as the **Transfer Function**, denoted universally as $G(s)$. The transfer function establishes the algebraic relationship between the Laplace transform of the output response $Y(s)$ and the Laplace transform of the input excitation $R(s)$, under the strict assumption of **zero initial conditions**. For a general SISO (Single-Input Single-Output) control system, the transfer function takes the canonical **Rational Polynomial Form**: $$ G(s) \;=\; \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} \;=\; \frac{b_0 \, s^{m} \;+\; b_1 \, s^{m-1} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; b_{m-1} \, s \;+\; b_m}{a_0 \, s^{n} \;+\; a_1 \, s^{n-1} \;+\; \cdots \;+\; a_{n-1} \, s \;+\; a_n} $$ where $a_i$ and $b_j$ are **real-valued constants** determined by the physical parameters (resistance, inductance, capacitance, mass, damping, stiffness) of the system. The variable $s$ is the **complex Laplace operator**, $s = \sigma + j\omega$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > The **poles** of a transfer function are defined as the values of $s$ for which the transfer function becomes **infinite (approaches $\infty$)**. Equivalently, poles are the roots of the characteristic equation ŌĆö the denominator polynomial set equal to zero. > > The **zeros** of a transfer function are defined as the values of $s$ for which the transfer function becomes **zero**. Equivalently, zeros are the roots of the numerator polynomial set equal to zero. ## 1.2 Decomposition into Pole-Zero Form (Factored Form) The rational polynomial $G(s)$ can be elegantly rewritten in the **Pole-Zero-Gain** canonical representation by factoring both the numerator and denominator completely: $$ G(s) \;=\; K \cdot \frac{(s - z_1)(s - z_2)(s - z_3)\cdots(s - z_m)}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)(s - p_3)\cdots(s - p_n)} $$ where the symbol meanings are: - $K$ = **DC Gain / System Gain Constant** (scalar multiplier, a real constant) - $z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_m$ = **Zeros of the system** (m zeros, since numerator is degree $m$) - $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n$ = **Poles of the system** (n poles, since denominator is degree $n$) > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Insight (Frequently Tested):** > Note the sign in the factors. A pole $p_k$ produces the factor $(s - p_k)$. If we substitute $s = p_k$ into the denominator, we get $(p_k - p_k) = 0$, which makes $G(s) \to \infty$ ŌĆö confirming the pole definition. The same logic applies inversely to zeros. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Mountain & Valley" Mental Model > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: A Hiking Terrain Map** > > Imagine the complex $s$-plane as a vast 3D hilly terrain where the height at every coordinate $(s)$ represents the **magnitude** of the transfer function $|G(s)|$. Then: > > - **Zeros** behave like **valleys** ŌĆö locations where the height touches the ground ($|G(s)| = 0$). If you stand exactly at a zero, the terrain elevation is zero. Walking slightly away, the elevation rises smoothly. > > - **Poles** behave like **infinite cliffs / chimneys** ŌĆö locations where the terrain shoots vertically to infinity ($|G(s)| \to \infty$). The closer you walk to a pole, the steeper the terrain becomes. > > - **The DC Gain $K$** is the **overall scale** of the map ŌĆö how tall the entire landscape is set to be. > > - **Poles and Zeros together** uniquely sculpt the entire frequency response shape ŌĆö the **Bode plot**, **Nyquist plot**, and **transient time response** are all encoded in their positions. ## 1.4 Physical Significance ŌĆö Why Do Poles and Zeros Matter? | Concept | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | **Poles** | Govern the **natural / intrinsic response** of the system (how the system behaves when input is removed, e.g., free vibration of a spring-mass-damper). | | **Zeros** | Govern the **forced response characteristics** (relative weighting of different modes; can cancel modes or shape overshoot). | | **Order of System** ($n$) | The number of independent energy-storage elements (e.g., inductors, capacitors, masses, springs) in the physical system. | | **Pole Locations** | Directly determine **stability, speed of response, oscillations, damping**. | | **Zero Locations** | Directly determine **transmission zeros, response magnitudes, notch behavior**. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Stability Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** > A linear continuous-time system is **BIBO Stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output Stable) **if and only if all poles lie strictly in the Left-Half of the s-plane**, meaning $\text{Re}(p_k) < 0 \; \forall k$. Any pole on the imaginary axis ($j\omega$-axis) implies **marginal stability** (sustained oscillations), and any pole in the Right-Half Plane (RHP) implies **instability** (unbounded response). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic To extract poles and zeros from a given transfer function, the engineer follows a rigorous algorithmic procedure: **Step 1 ŌĆö Acquire the Transfer Function $G(s)$** Ensure $G(s)$ is expressed as a ratio of two polynomials in descending powers of $s$ (standard form). Convert physical differential equations into the $s$-domain via Laplace transform, replacing $\frac{d}{dt} \to s$, $\frac{d^2}{dt^2} \to s^2$, etc. **Step 2 ŌĆö Equate the Denominator to Zero (Characteristic Equation)** The characteristic equation $D(s) = 0$ is the **soul of the system dynamics**. Solving $D(s) = 0$ yields all pole locations. This polynomial must always have at least one root (Fundamental Theorem of Algebra). **Step 3 ŌĆö Equate the Numerator to Zero** Solving $N(s) = 0$ yields all zero locations. Zeros may or may not exist (a system can have zero zeros). **Step 4 ŌĆö Classify Each Root by Type** - **Real Pole/Zero**: Single root on the real axis. - **Complex Pole/Zero Pair**: Complex roots always appear in **conjugate pairs** for real-coefficient polynomials. If $p_k = \alpha + j\beta$ is a pole, then $p_{k+1} = \alpha - j\beta$ is also a pole. - **Repeated Pole/Zero (Multiple Root)**: A root of multiplicity $r$ is called an $r$-th order pole/zero. **Step 5 ŌĆö Plot on the Complex s-Plane** Plot each pole with a cross symbol ($\times$) and each zero with a circle symbol ($\bigcirc$). This is the famous **Pole-Zero Map (PZ-Map)**. ## 2.2 Classification of Poles by Location | Pole Type | Location | Time-Domain Behavior | System Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **Stable Real Pole** $p = -\sigma$ ($\sigma > 0$) | LHP on real axis | $e^{-\sigma t}$ ŌĆö exponential decay | Stable, no oscillation | | **Stable Complex Pole Pair** $p = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ | LHP, away from real axis | $e^{-\sigma t}\sin(\omega_d t)$ ŌĆö decaying sinusoid | Stable, damped oscillation | | **Marginally Stable** $p = \pm j\omega$ | On imaginary axis | $\sin(\omega t)$ ŌĆö sustained oscillation | Marginal stability | | **Unstable Real Pole** $p = +\sigma$ ($\sigma > 0$) | RHP on real axis | $e^{+\sigma t}$ ŌĆö exponential growth | Unstable, unbounded | | **Unstable Complex Pair** $p = +\sigma \pm j\omega_d$ | RHP | $e^{+\sigma t}\sin(\omega_d t)$ ŌĆö growing oscillation | Unstable | ## 2.3 Effect of Poles on Time Response Consider a system with a single real pole at $s = -a$ (where $a > 0$). The impulse response is: $$ g(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s+a}\right\} \;=\; e^{-at} \cdot u(t) $$ The pole at $s = -a$ produces a mode that decays with **time constant** $\tau = \frac{1}{a}$. **Smaller $|a|$** (pole closer to imaginary axis) $\Rightarrow$ **slower decay** $\Rightarrow$ sluggish system. For a pair of complex conjugate poles at $s = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$: $$ g(t) \;=\; \frac{1}{\omega_d} \, e^{-\sigma t} \sin(\omega_d t) \cdot u(t) $$ The key physical parameters that emerge are: - **Damping Factor** $\sigma$ (exponential decay envelope). - **Damped Natural Frequency** $\omega_d$ (oscillation frequency). - **Undamped Natural Frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega_d^2}$. - **Damping Ratio** $\zeta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega_n}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pole-Zero Cancellation Rule:** > If a pole and zero share the **exact same location** in the $s$-plane, the corresponding factor $(s - z_k)/(s - p_k)$ simplifies to unity (provided they are of equal order). This is called **pole-zero cancellation**. In theory the mode is removed; in practice, imperfect cancellation can lead to **unstable hidden modes** (a famous robustness concern). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Identity | Expression | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | Transfer Function | $G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)}$ | Input-output relation in $s$-domain | | Pole Definition | $G(s) \to \infty$ when $s = p_k$ | $p_k$ are roots of denominator | | Zero Definition | $G(s) = 0$ when $s = z_k$ | $z_k$ are roots of numerator | | Pole-Zero-Gain Form | $G(s) = K \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{m}(s - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{n}(s - p_k)}$ | Factored canonical form | | Time Constant (Real Pole) | $\tau = \frac{1}{\vert p_k \vert}$ | Decay time for stable real pole | | Damping Factor | $\sigma = -\text{Re}(p_k)$ | Decay rate of complex pole pair | | Damped Frequency | $\omega_d = \text{Im}(p_k)$ | Oscillation frequency | | Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + \omega_d^2}$ | Undamped oscillation frequency | | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega_n} = \frac{-\text{Re}(p_k)}{\sqrt{[\text{Re}(p_k)]^2 + [\text{Im}(p_k)]^2}}$ | Oscillation damping | | BIBO Stability | $\text{Re}(p_k) < 0 \; \forall k$ | All poles strictly in LHP | | Order of System | $n = $ number of poles | Equals number of energy storages | | Pole-Zero Cancellation | $(s-p_k)/(s-p_k) = 1$ | Mode removal at matching locations | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > In **aerospace control** (aircraft autopilot design), pole placement via state feedback determines ride comfort and handling quality. In **electrical filter design**, zeros create notch filters to reject 50 Hz power-line interference. In **biomedical signal processing**, poles and zeros of ECG/ECG filters shape the QRS detection band. In **process control** (chemical plants), pole locations determine whether the temperature/pressure loops oscillate dangerously. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Comprehensive Example Problem ŌĆö Finding Poles, Zeros & Time Response > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked-Out Master Example (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE):** > > Given the transfer function: > $$ G(s) \;=\; \frac{2s^{2} + 6s + 4}{s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6} $$ > > **Task:** Find (i) Poles, (ii) Zeros, (iii) Pole-Zero Map description, (iv) Stability, (v) Impulse response $g(t)$. ### Step (i) ŌĆö Finding the Zeros Set the numerator polynomial equal to zero and solve for $s$: $$ N(s) \;=\; 2s^{2} + 6s + 4 \;=\; 0 $$ Factor out the common factor of 2: $$ 2(s^{2} + 3s + 2) \;=\; 0 $$ Divide both sides by 2 (non-zero scalar does not change roots): $$ s^{2} + 3s + 2 \;=\; 0 $$ Apply the standard quadratic formula $s = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^{2} - 4ac}}{2a}$, where $a=1$, $b=3$, $c=2$: $$ s \;=\; \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{3^{2} - 4(1)(2)}}{2(1)} $$ $$ s \;=\; \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{9 - 8}}{2} $$ $$ s \;=\; \frac{-3 \pm \sqrt{1}}{2} \;=\; \frac{-3 \pm 1}{2} $$ This yields two real solutions: $$ s_1 \;=\; \frac{-3 + 1}{2} \;=\; \frac{-2}{2} \;=\; -1 $$ $$ s_2 \;=\; \frac{-3 - 1}{2} \;=\; \frac{-4}{2} \;=\; -2 $$ **Conclusion for Zeros:** The system has **two real zeros** located at $\boxed{z_1 = -1}$ and $\boxed{z_2 = -2}$. Both zeros lie on the negative real axis in the Left-Half Plane (LHP). ### Step (ii) ŌĆö Finding the Poles Set the denominator polynomial equal to zero: $$ D(s) \;=\; s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6 \;=\; 0 $$ We attempt rational root candidates from the Rational Root Theorem: candidates are $\pm 1, \pm 2, \pm 3, \pm 6$. **Test $s = -1$:** Substitute and evaluate: $$ (-1)^{3} + 6(-1)^{2} + 11(-1) + 6 \;=\; -1 + 6 - 11 + 6 \;=\; 0 \;\;\checkmark $$ Since $s = -1$ is a root, factor out $(s + 1)$ via synthetic division of $D(s) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6$: $$ \begin{aligned} s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6 \;&=\; (s+1)(s^{2} + 5s + 6) \\ &=\; (s+1)(s+2)(s+3) \end{aligned} $$ **Verification:** Expand $(s+1)(s+2)(s+3) = (s+1)(s^2 + 5s + 6) = s^3 + 5s^2 + 6s + s^2 + 5s + 6 = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 \;\;\checkmark$ Thus the poles are: $$ \boxed{p_1 = -1, \quad p_2 = -2, \quad p_3 = -3} $$ ### Step (iii) ŌĆö Pole-Zero Map Plot all singularities on the complex $s$-plane: - **Zeros (circles $\bigcirc$):** at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$ (both on negative real axis). - **Poles (crosses $\times$):** at $s = -1$, $s = -2$, $s = -3$. ### Step (iv) ŌĆö Stability Analysis Inspect all three poles: $p_1 = -1$, $p_2 = -2$, $p_3 = -3$. All poles satisfy $\text{Re}(p_k) < 0$ (strictly negative real parts). By the **BIBO Stability Criterion**: $$ \text{Re}(p_k) < 0 \;\; \forall \; k \in \{1, 2, 3\} $$ **Conclusion:** The system is **asymptotically stable** (strictly stable). ### Step (v) ŌĆö Impulse Response $g(t)$ First perform **partial fraction decomposition (PFD)** of $G(s)$: $$ G(s) \;=\; \frac{2s^{2} + 6s + 4}{(s+1)(s+2)(s+3)} \;=\; \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2} + \frac{C}{s+3} $$ where $A$, $B$, $C$ are the **residues** to be found. **Solve for $A$:** Multiply both sides by $(s+1)$ and substitute $s = -1$ (Heaviside cover-up method): $$ A \;=\; \left.\frac{2s^{2} + 6s + 4}{(s+2)(s+3)}\right|_{s = -1} $$ $$ A \;=\; \frac{2(-1)^{2} + 6(-1) + 4}{(-1+2)(-1+3)} \;=\; \frac{2 - 6 + 4}{(1)(2)} \;=\; \frac{0}{2} \;=\; 0 $$ **Solve for $B$:** Multiply both sides by $(s+2)$ and substitute $s = -2$: $$ B \;=\; \left.\frac{2s^{2} + 6s + 4}{(s+1)(s+3)}\right|_{s = -2} $$ $$ B \;=\; \frac{2(-2)^{2} + 6(-2) + 4}{(-2+1)(-2+3)} \;=\; \frac{8 - 12 + 4}{(-1)(1)} \;=\; \frac{0}{-1} \;=\; 0 $$ **Solve for $C$:** Multiply both sides by $(s+3)$ and substitute $s = -3$: $$ C \;=\; \left.\frac{2s^{2} + 6s + 4}{(s+1)(s+2)}\right|_{s = -3} $$ $$ C \;=\; \frac{2(-3)^{2} + 6(-3) + 4}{(-3+1)(-3+2)} \;=\; \frac{18 - 18 + 4}{(-2)(-1)} \;=\; \frac{4}{2} \;=\; 2 $$ Therefore, the partial fraction decomposition yields: $$ G(s) \;=\; \frac{0}{s+1} + \frac{0}{s+2} + \frac{2}{s+3} \;=\; \frac{2}{s+3} $$ **Interpretation:** The poles at $s = -1$ and $s = -2$ are **completely cancelled by zeros at the same locations**! The system effectively behaves like a **first-order system** with a single pole at $s = -3$. Now apply the standard inverse Laplace transform pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at} u(t)$: $$ g(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\left\{\frac{2}{s+3}\right\} \;=\; 2 \, e^{-3t} \, u(t) $$ **Final impulse response:** $$ \boxed{g(t) \;=\; 2 \, e^{-3t} \, u(t)} $$ where $u(t)$ is the unit step function (Heaviside function). ## 3.2 Analytical Insight from the Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Pedagogical Lessons:** > 1. The **order of the transfer function** (3rd order, here) does **not** equal the order of the effective system (1st order here) ŌĆö cancellations reduce the effective order. > 2. Pole-zero cancellation is a **design intent** or a **numerical accident** ŌĆö both must be examined carefully for **hidden unstable modes** (in physical realizations). > 3. The impulse response decay rate is governed by the **dominant pole** (closest to imaginary axis among non-cancelled poles). Here, the only effective pole $p_3 = -3$ gives $\tau = 1/3$ seconds. ## 3.3 Derivations of Key Damping Parameters For a second-order characteristic polynomial with complex conjugate poles $p_{1,2} = -\sigma \pm j\omega_d$, the quadratic factor is: $$ (s + \sigma - j\omega_d)(s + \sigma + j\omega_d) \;=\; s^{2} + 2\sigma s + (\sigma^{2} + \omega_d^{2}) $$ Comparing with the standard second-order form $s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^{2}$: $$ 2\zeta\omega_n \;=\; 2\sigma \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta\omega_n \;=\; \sigma $$ $$ \omega_n^{2} \;=\; \sigma^{2} + \omega_d^{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n \;=\; \sqrt{\sigma^{2} + \omega_d^{2}} $$ $$ \zeta \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\omega_n} \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + \omega_d^{2}}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Damping Ratio Classification:** > - $\zeta = 0$: Undamped (poles on $j\omega$-axis, sustained oscillation). > - $0 < \zeta < 1$: **Underdamped** (complex conjugate poles in LHP). > - $\zeta = 1$: **Critically damped** (repeated real pole at $s = -\omega_n$). > - $\zeta > 1$: **Overdamped** (two distinct real poles on negative real axis). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pole-Zero Map of the Worked Example ```mermaid graph TB subgraph sPlane["Complex s-Plane ŌĆö Pole-Zero Map"] axis["Imaginary Axis jŽē (vertical)"] realAxis["Real Axis Žā (horizontal)"] pole1["├Ś p1 = -1
