### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Lead-Lag Compensator Frequency Analysis

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A lead-lag compensator has the transfer function $G_{c}(s) = \frac{(1+0.5s)(1+2s)}{(1+0.1s)(1+20s)}$. Identify the lead and lag sections, determine the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and sketch (describe) the pole-zero configuration on the s-plane.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For the same lead-lag compensator, compute the maximum phase lead of the lead section, the maximum phase lag of the lag section, and the net phase contribution at $\omega = 1$ rad/s. State whether this compensator will improve transient or steady-state response (or both).

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆö Identification and Pole-Zero Plot:**

Comparing $G_c(s) = \frac{(1+0.5s)(1+2s)}{(1+0.1s)(1+20s)}$ with standard form $\frac{(1+T_1 s)(1+T_2 s)}{(1+\alpha T_1 s)(1+\beta T_2 s)}$:

**Lead Section:** $\frac{1+0.5s}{1+0.1s}$ ŌåÆ $T_1 = 0.5$ s, $\alpha T_1 = 0.1$ s ŌåÆ $\alpha = 0.1/0.5 = 0.2$.

**Lag Section:** $\frac{1+2s}{1+20s}$ ŌåÆ $T_2 = 2$ s, $\beta T_2 = 20$ s ŌåÆ $\beta = 20/2 = 10$.

**Pole-Zero Locations:**

| **Section** | **Pole** | **Zero** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Lead ($T_1 = 0.5$ s) | $s = -1/(0.1) = -10$ | $s = -1/(0.5) = -2$ |
| Lag ($T_2 = 2$ s) | $s = -1/(20) = -0.05$ | $s = -1/(2) = -0.5$ |

**Pole-Zero Pattern (from origin ŌåÆ leftward):**

$$-0.05 \text{ (pole lag)} \rightarrow -0.5 \text{ (zero lag)} \rightarrow -2 \text{ (zero lead)} \rightarrow -10 \text{ (pole lead)}$$

This confirms the expected geometry: lag pole closest to origin, lead pole farthest from origin.

**Part (b) ŌĆö Phase Calculations:**

**Maximum phase lead of lead section** ($\alpha = 0.2$):

$$\phi_{lead,max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1 - 0.2}{1 + 0.2}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.8}{1.2}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.6667) = 41.8^\circ$$

**Maximum phase lag of lag section** ($\beta = 10$):

$$\phi_{lag,max} = -\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10 - 1}{10 + 1}\right) = -\sin^{-1}(0.8182) = -54.9^\circ$$

**Net phase at $\omega = 1$ rad/s:**

$$\phi(1) = \tan^{-1}(0.5) - \tan^{-1}(0.1) + \tan^{-1}(2) - \tan^{-1}(20)$$

Computing each term:
- $\tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57┬░$
- $\tan^{-1}(0.1) = 5.71┬░$
- $\tan^{-1}(2) = 63.43┬░$
- $\tan^{-1}(20) = 87.14┬░$

$$\phi(1) = 26.57┬░ - 5.71┬░ + 63.43┬░ - 87.14┬░ = -2.85┬░$$

**Conclusion:** This compensator provides substantial **phase lead** at higher frequencies (improving transient response and stability margins) and significant **phase lag** at lower frequencies (improving steady-state error via gain redistribution). The net phase at $\omega = 1$ rad/s is slightly negative, but at higher frequencies (e.g., $\omega = 5$ rad/s) the lead action dominates. Therefore, this compensator **improves both transient and steady-state response** ŌĆö the hallmark benefit of a lead-lag network.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Phase Formulas:** Students frequently write $\phi_{max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\beta-1}{\beta+1}\right)$ for the lead network or $\phi_{max} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right)$ for the lag network. **Remember:** Lead uses $\alpha$ in the numerator $(1-\alpha)$, Lag uses $\beta$ in the numerator $(\beta-1)$. The lag phase is **negative**.
> 
> 2. **Pole-Zero Order:** For lag, students often incorrectly state the pole is to the *left* of the zero. The correct answer is: **for lag, pole is to the right of zero (closer to origin). For lead, pole is to the left of zero (farther from origin).**
> 
> 3. **Magnitude Boost vs. Attenuation:** Lead networks **amplify** at high frequencies; lag networks **attenuate** at high frequencies. Confusing these is a guaranteed 1ŌĆō2 mark deduction.
> 
> 4. **Units of $\omega_m$:** Always state $\omega_m$ in **rad/s**. Omitting units loses 0.5 marks.
> 
> 5. **Confusing $T$ and $\alpha T$:** The time constant $T$ corresponds to the zero location $-1/T$, while $\alpha T$ (or $\beta T$) corresponds to the pole location. Mixing these up is a common error.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 ŌĆö Lag, Lead, Lead-Lag Networks:**
> 
> - **Lag Compensator:** $G(s) = \frac{1+Ts}{1+\beta Ts}$, $\beta > 1$. **Pole closer to origin** than zero. **Phase is negative (lag).** Attenuates high frequencies. Improves **steady-state error**.
> - **Lead Compensator:** $G(s) = \frac{1+Ts}{1+\alpha Ts}$, $0 < \alpha < 1$. **Zero closer to origin** than pole. **Phase is positive (lead).** Amplifies high frequencies. Improves **transient response** (phase margin).
> - **Lead-Lag Compensator:** $G(s) = \frac{(1+T_1 s)(1+T_2 s)}{(1+\alpha T_1 s)(1+\beta T_2 s)}$. Combines both. $T_1 < T_2$ typically. Improves **both transient and steady-state**.
> - **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
>   - $\omega_{m,lead} = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\alpha}}$
>   - $\omega_{m,lag} = \dfrac{1}{T\sqrt{\beta}}$
>   - $\phi_{max,lead} = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha}\right)$
>   - $\phi_{max,lag} = -\sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\beta-1}{\beta+1}\right)$
>   - Magnitude boost/attenuation = $\pm 10\log_{10}(\cdot)$ dB
> - **Electrical Realization:** Lag = series $R$ + parallel $RC$ at output. Lead = series $R$ + capacitor at output.
> - **Pole-Zero Geometry Rule:** **L**ag = **L**arger pole magnitude? NO! Lag = pole **L**eft-shifted? NO! The correct rule is: **In LAG, the pole is to the Right of the zero (Lagging behind on the real axis).** **In LEAD, the pole is to the Left of the zero (Leading forward).**
> - **No Design in This Module:** You are *not* required to compute $\alpha$, $\beta$, or $T$ for a given specification. Only the structural analysis, transfer function interpretation, and frequency response derivation are in scope.
> - **Geometric Frequency $\omega_m$:** Always between the pole and zero corner frequencies on a logarithmic scale.
> - **DC Gain:** All three compensators have unity DC gain in their normalized form. The constant offset ($1/\beta$ or $1/\alpha$) is absorbed into the system gain $K$.
